laravel-rome.name: laravel-rome
laravel-rome.tagline: Database views as first-class Eloquent citizens.
laravel-rome.description: Laravel Rome brings read-only models backed by database views into your Eloquent layer — with zero query duplication, native multi-tenant support, and a driver abstraction that speaks your dialect. When your reporting logic belongs in the database, Rome makes sure Eloquent knows it.

sections.alt: HasReadOnlyMode trait added to an existing Eloquent model
sections.title: Retrofit any model — no rewrite needed.
sections.body: Add a single trait to any existing Eloquent model and unlock two new access patterns: block all write operations on an instance, or redirect reads to a dedicated view — without touching your schema, your migrations, or your class hierarchy.

sections.alt: ReadOnlyModel class definition extending Rome's base class
sections.title: ReadOnlyModel — Eloquent, but honest.
sections.body: Extend the read-only base class instead of the standard model and your class declares its intent: this data is read from a view, never written back. No accidental inserts, no phantom dirty states. Your domain stays clean.

sections.alt: Materialized view refresh API usage
sections.title: Materialized view refresh, on your terms.
sections.body: Schedule or trigger refresh cycles from Laravel — no raw SQL scattered across your codebase. Rome exposes a clean API to keep your materialized views fresh, integrated with the job and scheduler infrastructure you already have.

inertia-split.name: inertia-split
inertia-split.tagline: One controller. One route. Inertia and JSON, both.
inertia-split.description: inertia-split lets you serve an Inertia SPA and a JSON API from the same Laravel controller action — no branching logic, no duplicate routes, no separate API layer. Build hybrid endpoints from day one, or annotate an existing API controller method by method as you adopt Inertia incrementally.

sections.alt: HasHybridResponses trait used in a Laravel controller
sections.title: Hybrid responses — zero branching.
sections.body: Add a single trait to any controller and return a hybrid response. Chain a component for SPA requests, or a route for redirects. When an API client hits the same endpoint, it gets plain JSON back. The controller doesn't branch — inertia-split does.

sections.alt: #[InertiaComponent] PHP attribute on an existing controller method
sections.title: Incremental migration — annotate, don't rewrite.
sections.body: Already have a working Laravel API? Annotate any method with a single attribute and leave the body untouched. Inertia requests get a rendered component with your data as props. API clients get the same JSON they always got. Methods without the attribute are completely unaffected.

sections.alt: Split facade usage example
sections.title: Three ways to use it — pick yours.
sections.body: Use the trait, extend the base controller to skip the use declaration entirely, or reach for the facade from anywhere in your application. Same behaviour, your preferred style.

laravel-nudge.name: laravel-nudge
laravel-nudge.tagline: Give notifications a lifecycle. They resolve themselves.
laravel-nudge.description: A notification is not just a message — it is a pending state tied to an expected future action. laravel-nudge lets a notification declare the action it is waiting on, and when that action runs anywhere in your application, the notification resolves itself. No manual wiring. No coupling between the sender, the handler, and the notification.

sections.alt: Diagram showing a notification resolving when its expected action fires
sections.title: The concept — pending state, not just a message.
sections.body: A notification declares the action it's waiting on. When that action executes — via a webhook, a CLI command, an OAuth callback, anywhere — an event fires, the listener finds the matching notification, and stamps it resolved. The controller, the handler, and the notification have no knowledge of each other.

sections.alt: ResolvableAction implementation with DispatchesActionExecuted trait
sections.title: Actions — implement, call, forget.
sections.body: Implement the resolvable-action contract, pull in the dispatching trait, and put your logic in the handler. Call it publicly. The event is dispatched automatically after the handler returns — and only if it succeeds. You never touch the notification layer from inside the action.

sections.alt: ActionableNotification with forAction() and useActionKey() usage examples
sections.title: Notifications — two ways to bind an action.
sections.body: Extend the actionable notification and supply the action key either at the call site, or baked into the class itself. Param matching is subset-based: store only the params that must match, and the resolver ignores everything else.

sections.alt: HasResolvableNotifications trait with pendingNotifications and resolvedNotifications scopes
sections.title: Query pending and resolved — out of the box.
sections.body: Add a single trait to your notifiable model and get pending- and resolved-notification scopes instantly. Or query the resolved timestamp directly. The migration adds the column to your existing notifications table — no new table, no new model.

laravel-depth.name: laravel-depth
laravel-depth.tagline: Know the blast radius before you touch anything.
laravel-depth.description: laravel-depth is a static analysis tool for Laravel that traces dependency caller trees through constructor injection. Give it a class suffix and it walks backward through your entire codebase — showing you exactly what depends on what, where the entry points are, and which classes have no callers at all. Built for architects doing audits, and developers about to refactor something they didn't write.

sections.alt: php artisan depth:trace QueryService terminal output
sections.title: One command. Your entire dependency tree.
sections.code:
php artisan depth:trace QueryService

# JSON output
php artisan depth:trace QueryService --json

# Write to file
php artisan depth:trace QueryService --output=storage/app/depth-report.txt
sections.body: Point the trace command at any class and laravel-depth scans your codebase, builds a reverse dependency index from constructor type hints, and traces every caller upward to its entry point — controller, job, command, or listener. No runtime, no container inspection, pure static analysis.

sections.alt: Tree output showing callers, entry points, and orphan classes
sections.title: Entry points, cycles, and orphans — all flagged.
sections.code:
App\Services\FooQueryService
 └── App\UseCases\FooUseCase
      └── App\Http\Controllers\FooController
          [ENTRY: GET api/foo → api, auth]

⚠  ORPHAN: App\Services\OrphanQueryService
sections.body: Traversal stops at configurable entry-point suffixes and enriches controller nodes with their route method, URI, and middleware stack. Cycles are marked to avoid infinite recursion. Classes with no callers are surfaced as orphans — the dead code your codebase forgot to remove.

sections.alt: depth:trace with --uses and --grep filter flags
sections.title: Filter by what they use, not just what they're named.
sections.code:
# Only classes whose source contains '@deprecated'
php artisan depth:trace Repository --grep='@deprecated'

# Only classes using a specific contract or trait
php artisan depth:trace Repository --uses=Auditable --uses=TenantScoped
sections.body: Narrow results to match plain text in source files, or filter by trait, interface, parent class, or method parameter type. Useful when auditing a codebase you didn't write — find every class that uses a deprecated contract or inherits from a base you're about to replace.

sections.alt: JSON output from depth:trace with route and middleware metadata
sections.title: Machine-readable output for your own tooling.
sections.body: Ask for JSON and laravel-depth outputs a structured tree you can pipe into reports, dashboards, or CI checks. Write it straight to a file. The same data that powers the human-readable tree — entry points, cycles, orphans, route metadata — fully available for automation.

laravel-enum-friendly.name: laravel-enum-friendly
laravel-enum-friendly.tagline: Everything PHP enums should have shipped with.
laravel-enum-friendly.description: A trait you drop into any enum and immediately get 25+ methods for form integration, TypeScript generation, validation rules, safe coercion, filtering, and more. Built on a dependency-free core so the same utilities work outside Laravel too — in Symfony, CLI tools, microservices, or plain PHP.

sections.alt: ExtendedEnum trait added to a Laravel enum
sections.title: One trait. Twenty-five methods.
sections.body: Add the ExtendedEnum trait to any backed or unbacked enum and unlock the full method surface instantly. No configuration, no service provider setup beyond installation, no base class to extend. If you already have enums, you're one use statement away.

sections.alt: toSelectOptions() returning a Laravel Collection for use in a Blade or Inertia form
sections.title: Form-ready, out of the box.
sections.body: toSelectOptions() returns a Laravel Collection of value/label pairs ready to drop into any select element — Blade, Inertia, Livewire, or API response. No manual array_column(), no custom mappers. The enum is the source of truth for your frontend too.

sections.alt: UserStatus::rules() used inside a Laravel Form Request
sections.title: Validation rules — generated, not written.
sections.body: Call rules() on any enum to get a complete Laravel validation rule array — type constraint and in: check included, derived directly from the enum cases. No magic strings, no drift between your enum definition and your validation logic.

sections.alt: toTypeScript() output used to generate a TypeScript union type
sections.title: TypeScript sync — no manual type files.
sections.body: toTypeScript() outputs a structured representation of your enum that a generator script can turn into TypeScript union types. Keep your PHP enum as the single source of truth and generate frontend types from it — no duplication, no drift.

sections.alt: EnumFriendly Core trait used in a plain PHP project with no framework
sections.title: Framework-agnostic core — use it anywhere.
sections.body: The full utility layer is extracted into a dependency-free core package — no Laravel, no Symfony, nothing. Use the same enum methods in any PHP 8.1+ project. The Laravel package builds on top of it, adding Collections, validation rules, and Artisan commands without changing the core API.

splitstack.name: splitstack
splitstack.tagline: Start every project with the hard parts already done.
splitstack.description: A Laravel + Svelte 5 starter template with end-to-end type safety, built-in multi-tenancy, DDD architecture, and real-time DB events — no broker required. Not a boilerplate that gives you a running app. A typed pipeline from database to UI, with the structural decisions already made.

sections.alt: Translucid watchCollection in a Svelte component reacting to PostgreSQL NOTIFY
sections.title: Real-time — powered by PostgreSQL, not Redis.
sections.body: Translucid uses PostgreSQL's native LISTEN/NOTIFY to push DB changes to your frontend the moment they happen — no Redis, no Pusher, no extra process to manage. Add the trait, install the trigger once, and your Svelte components react to row-level events. Multi-tenant aware: each tenant's events are scoped to their own private channel.

sections.alt: DDD application layer folder structure with Actions, UseCases, Queries, DTOs
sections.title: DDD architecture — scaffolded, not theorized.
sections.body: Actions, UseCases, Queries, DTOs, Entities, Value Objects, and Repositories — all scaffolded by convention, not manually wired. Repositories are auto-bound to their interfaces by naming convention. DTOs extend BaseDTO and get serialization helpers for free. The structure enforces the right dependency direction from day one.

sections.alt: Perspective class resolving role-variant UI config in a Svelte component
sections.title: RBAC UI — config-driven, not conditional.
sections.body: Perspective is a typed RBAC config system that eliminates scattered role conditionals from your components. Define what each role sees once, resolve it by role string anywhere. Navigation, table columns, available actions, visible sections — all driven by config, all statically typed.

sections.alt: packages/splitstack directory listing Translucid, Stashable, Metamon, EnumFriendly
sections.title: In-repo packages — extract when ready.
sections.code:
packages/splitstack/
├── translucid/          # PG LISTEN/NOTIFY real-time eventing
├── laravel-stashable/   # Attribute-driven repository caching
│   #[WithCache(ttl: 300, key: 'orders.{id}')]
│   public function findById(int $id): Order { … }
├── laravel-metamon/     # Typed JSON metadata on Eloquent models
│   $model->meta('settings.theme', 'dark');
└── laravel-enum-friendly/ # Enum utilities (also a standalone package)
sections.body: Splitstack ships with four internal packages that live in packages/splitstack/ and are usable as standalone Composer packages. Stashable gives you attribute-driven repository caching with tenant-aware cache keys. Metamon turns any JSON column into a dot-notation metadata API. Each can be extracted and published independently as your project grows.
